title: Retained Baby Teeth in Dogs: Managing the 'Double Row' of Teeth
meta_desc: Find out why dogs get a double row of teeth, the risks of retained baby teeth, and why it is a low-urgency veterinary concern.
content_html:
What are Retained Baby Teeth?

Retained deciduous teeth, commonly referred to as 'shark teeth,' occur when a puppy's primary (baby) teeth do not fall out to make room for the permanent adult teeth. Instead of the adult tooth pushing the baby tooth out, the adult tooth erupts alongside it, creating a double row. This is most common in smaller breeds but can happen to any dog during their teething phase.

Is it an Emergency?

The urgency level for retained baby teeth is Low. This is not an immediate medical emergency that requires an ER visit. However, it is a condition that must be addressed by your veterinarian during a scheduled appointment. If left untreated, the extra teeth can cause overcrowding, trapping food and bacteria, which leads to premature periodontal disease, gum infection, and the misalignment of the adult bite.

Why a Photo Helps Triage

Taking a clear photo of your dog's mouth is incredibly helpful for a veterinary professional. A photo allows the triage team to see exactly which teeth are affected and check for signs of secondary issues like red, inflamed gums or heavy tartar buildup. This information helps the vet determine if the extraction can wait until your pet's scheduled spay or neuter surgery or if a separate dental procedure should be prioritized sooner.

faqs.question: At what age should a dog's baby teeth be gone?
faqs.answer: Most puppies should lose all of their baby teeth by the time they are 6 to 7 months old.

faqs.question: Can I pull the baby tooth out myself?
faqs.answer: No, you should never attempt to pull a dog's tooth at home. The roots of baby teeth are very long and fragile; breaking them can cause infection or damage the developing adult tooth.

faqs.question: Will the baby tooth eventually fall out on its own?
faqs.answer: Once the adult tooth has fully erupted alongside the baby tooth, it is very unlikely the baby tooth will fall out naturally because the root is no longer being properly absorbed.
